ocar.opportunity: While partial mushroom body connectomes had been described for larva and fragments of adult, a complete synaptic connectivity map of the entire adult Drosophila mushroom body — with all Kenyon cells, DANs, MBONs, and interneurons — had not been assembled into a unified circuit model.
ocar.challenge: Extracting the complete mushroom body circuit from the hemibrain EM dataset required careful proofreading of thousands of densely packed Kenyon cell axons in the pedunculus and lobes, and systematic identification and typing of all cell types based on their morphological and connectivity signatures.
ocar.action: The authors extracted and comprehensively analyzed the complete adult mushroom body circuit from the hemibrain connectome, identifying all ~2000 Kenyon cells, 20 DAN types, ~34 MBON types, and interneurons, and building a detailed circuit model from their connectivity.
ocar.resolution: The complete adult MB connectome revealed previously uncharacterized feedback connectivity, identified compartment-specific circuit logic for different memory types (acquisition, extinction, consolidation), and provided circuit-level predictions for how the MB supports value-based learning. The study found that MBONs directly synapse onto DANs, forming recurrent loops that could sustain memory states.
ocar.future_work: Future work should use the connectome as a scaffold for biophysical modeling of memory dynamics and test whether the identified feedback circuits contribute to memory consolidation or extinction in behavioral experiments.

summaries.beginner: Fruit flies learn to avoid smells associated with punishment. This study completely mapped all the nerve cells in the fly's memory center from the hemibrain dataset, revealing the full circuit diagram. This allowed scientists to build models of how the fly learns from experience and predict new things about how memory works.
summaries.intermediate: The complete adult Drosophila mushroom body connectome from the hemibrain dataset identifies all cell types and their connectivity, revealing recurrent DAN-MBON loops and compartment-specific circuit logic that extends previous partial models. The circuit architecture predicts computational mechanisms for memory acquisition, consolidation, and extinction, generating testable hypotheses for functional studies.
summaries.advanced: This comprehensive analysis of the hemibrain MB circuit identifies previously uncharacterized DAN-MBON reciprocal connections forming closed-loop circuits within compartments, which could implement temporal credit assignment or memory persistence. The study reveals that MBONs from adjacent compartments converge onto the same downstream targets, suggesting integration across memory valence compartments. A key methodological advance is the systematic cell-type assignment based on connectivity fingerprints rather than morphology alone. Caveats include single-animal snapshot limitations and that the hemibrain covers only one side of the brain.

discussion_prompts: What new computational functions do the DAN-MBON recurrent loops in the adult MB circuit support that were not predicted from the larval connectome or prior functional work?
How does the compartment-specific circuit architecture revealed by the complete adult MB connectome map onto the known behavioral dissociations between different MB lobe compartments?
What predictions from the adult MB circuit model are most amenable to direct experimental testing, and which are most likely to be validated versus revised?
